summary: Uncle Bob responds to an Atlantic article about the 'software apocalypse' that blamed software failures on inadequate tools rather than programmer discipline. He criticizes the article for interviewing only tool inventors while ignoring thought leaders like Kent Beck and Martin Fowler who focus on craftsmanship. Using a scene from the TV show 'Wisdom of the Crowd' as an illustration, he argues that the real cause of software disasters is programmers taking shortcuts under pressure and others providing cover for sloppy work. He dismisses the idea that better REPLs, model-driven engineering, or other tools will solve the problem, emphasizing instead that disciplined testing practices and professional accountability are the true solutions.
key_points: Tools like Light Table, Model Driven Engineering, and TLA+ are useful but cannot solve the fundamental problem of undisciplined programming
The software apocalypse stems from two causes: programmers taking sloppy shortcuts under schedule pressure, and other programmers providing cover for that behavior
REPLs allow you to 'try' things but not 'test' them—tests are repeatable, reviewable documents that can be enhanced and added to suites
The Atlantic article failed to even consider that programmers doing a better job might prevent catastrophic bugs
In a room full of programmers, fewer than one in twenty regularly write unit tests
money_quotes: Tools are fine; but the solution to the software apocalypse is not more tools. The solution is better programming discipline.
The author completely avoided the notion that the software apocalypse might be avoided if programmers just did a better job.
The author seemed convinced that the solution to the software apocalypse – the solution to bad code – is more code.
I knew I shouldn't have reused that code, but we were in a rush.
You don't test things in the REPL; you try things in the REPL. A test is much more formal than a trial.
A test can be repeated. A test can be enhanced. You can add to a test. You can review a test. A test is a document. A test is a program.
The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, But in ourselves…
How many of you write unit tests on a regular basis? Not one in twenty raised their hands.
key_insight: The software industry's pursuit of better tools distracts from the real solution to catastrophic bugs: raising the level of programmer discipline and refusing to excuse sloppy work done under schedule pressure.
